item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: EDIT: Just noting here that I ended up revising the last section of this SCP. Hopefully it addresses some of the concerns people had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm… a little iffy on this. Initially I was strongly leaning for an upvote, but the last addendum…

The last addendum is basically a lot of technobabble which only says "This is a compulsion effect." There is no additional meaning in the language you used, just getting that exact same idea across. It's capable of psychically physiologically-ish compelling them to silence and complacency through magic anomalous action at a distance.

Kind of the same idea, telling us the mechanism for a compulsion effect or pointing out that it can be resisted without changing the outcomes doesn't improve anything. (For a discussion of why this is a problem, including how it pertains to this article, see this comment by someone smarter than me.)

I don't think it would even have been such a dig deal if it hadn't been your last-addendum security-locked 'twist.' As it is, it feels like you just used a whole addendum to hang a big banner on the weakest part of your article.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you liked it! I noted that it's orbiting SCP-3599-2 later in the description because I didn't want to bring it up at the start and only classify it at the end, but it seems like that's caused some confusion. I probably could have gone into more detail on the qualities of its orbit, but that didn't seem particularly meaningful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this, and you've got a +1, but one note: You refer to this as an orbital station, but never say what it's orbiting. At a distance of 16 light years, it's probably not our sun: at those ranges an elliptical orbit would be better described as "drifting in space with a similar vector to our solar system". Highly eccentric orbits would be implausible at that distance, too. At perigee you'd either be screaming past the sun at speeds best measured in percentages of the speed of light or way beyond the heliosphere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One thing I like about some SCPs is reading the containment procedures and trying to guess what on (or off) Earth could they apply to.
This delivered that challenge nicely.

The actual anomaly also satisfied. I'm one of the few people I know who has actually used a punch clock. The ambiguity of what, exactly, is being manufactured, by whom, and why, was a big part of my enjoyment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid I don't entirely understand what you're asking about the use of C-Class personnel. That might be because of a blind spot to some idea I internalized while writing the piece or not, but I'm not sure what to say.

As to the point about it shifting from an interesting idea to a fairly generic one, I hadn't intended the second addendum to change the meaning/themes/etc. of the piece that drastically. I assume most of that tonal shift is from the last line? I don't think it would detract from the piece to remove it if that's something other people felt as well, especially given the similarities to SCP-1548 that I'm seeing after reading TL333s' reply. (EDIT: I just went ahead and did this after looking back over it. That line was definitely out of place in the rest of the piece.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's quirky and whimsical, but I think I'm being more entertained by the thought that the Foreman is the Hateful Star's brother. Like, it's not his fault he's successful, if only Hateful would get out of their parents' house now and then maybe he could make something of himself, but of course, once he does, he has to go and make a big production of it because he's a drama queen. What a star child.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I really like this idea, but your execution needs work.

You don't explain a lot of what you talk about in your description. For instance, are the c-class personnel the people the foundation considered C-class?

This…unfortunately feels a bit formulaic beyond the actual idea. Anomaly kidnaps people and forces them to work for glorious space capitalist? Cool. Anomaly is on its way to DESTROY THE EARTH, RAWR. Kind of boring.

This gets my upvote for being technically proficient, and a good idea, but it suffers with some pacing and execution problems.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This got me laughing, in a positive way, at the absurdity. I upvoted at

At the conclusion of a shift, SCP-3599-1 terminals dispense payment at the rate of $5.35 per hour.

because of course the space capitalists don't even pay minimum wage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And thank you for your feedback on it there! I knew I was forgetting to mention something in my post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I remember commenting on this in the forums. Great to see it on the mainsite! Enthusiastic +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for space SCPs, and this one was interesting. To be perfectly honest, I did not see that twist coming at the end.

item.title: Author Note
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to DrMagnus for making the addendum formatting I used available. It really scratched an aesthetic itch of mine.

I don't think I have any more of interest to say.

Translations:
Chinese: breaddddd
